chapters.title: There is no single normal, and that is the starting point
chapters.text:
In high school, Thomas Erikson noticed he clicked with some people and not with others. He also wondered why some talks flowed and others did not. Without an answer at the time, he figured something was wrong with the people who did not get him, and he started to avoid them.

When Erikson was 25, he met a man named Sture. Sture had built a business from the ground up over many years. Erikson had to interview him for a project, and through the talk he saw that Sture felt surrounded by people who did not understand him. Sture was a successful businessman, but he could not read his own staff, even though he spoke with them often. Erikson decided he did not want to end up like that, and he began to study how to read people who at first seem hard to deal with.

In his research, Erikson found the DISA method, a way to describe and sort human behavior. Bill Bonnstetter and his son David built software around it, and that is the system Erikson uses in this summary. They ran their company, TTI Success Insights, on it. DISA stands for Dominance, Influence, Steadiness, and Compliance, the four main behavior styles that show how people see themselves in their world. Each style is tied to a color: Red, Yellow, Green, and Blue.

Knowing the theory is not enough. You get better when you turn it into real steps.

This book aims to unlock human potential. It also gives practical tips for talking with different kinds of people and helps you see those you once called idiots as people worth respect and understanding.

Read on for the differences between the four styles and how to use them.

chapters.title: Communication lands on the listener, not the speaker
chapters.text:
When you speak, the listener filters your words through their own biases and assumptions. What is left is what they actually hear, and that is why misunderstandings happen. You have little control over this part, no matter how clearly you try to speak. It is one of the built-in challenges of talking to others.

Most people already know how they want to be treated, so adjusting to their style helps them get your message. Being flexible and reading what others need is what makes a good communicator.

Psychoanalyst Carl Jung argued that the different behavior patterns we see across cultures are what make life dynamic. Behavior patterns are the set of attitudes, beliefs, and habits that guide how a person acts. We can often see our own patterns, but reading others is harder. Still, it is needed if you want to communicate well. The American Journal of Business Education has noted that more than 50 million people have been assessed with the DISA tool.

Everyone behaves differently. No matter how odd a person seems, they are normal in some setting. That is the basis of every strong human interaction.

chapters.title: Where your traits come from in the first place
chapters.text:
Long before birth, the seeds of how you act are already planted. Family lines pass down pieces of who you are, and during the early years, you copy whatever you see in the people around you. As years pass, you lock in core beliefs that feel almost impossible to shift, picked up from parents, family, and the first classrooms. Those deep beliefs shape how you act today.

A view of the world is just an opinion built from what you have lived through.

William Moulton Marston ran an early test of blood pressure during stress and used it to map how healthy adults behave. His work is the basis of the DISA model. The four letters stand for the four main styles. D is Dominance, which stirs action in a tough setting. I is Influence, which stirs action in a friendly setting. S is Steadiness, which brings calm in a friendly setting. A is Compliance, which brings calm in a tough setting.

Dominance, tied to the Red label, is about how a person tackles obstacles. Influence, the Yellow label, is about pulling others along. Steadiness, the Green label, shows how open someone is to change. Compliance, the Blue label, shows how strictly someone sticks to rules.

The Red style is first. The Greek doctor Hippocrates named this kind choleric. A Red type is bold, driven, quick to flare up, and likes to lead. Strong Red types get energy from other people, love chasing goals, push through roadblocks, and need some contest to feel alive. Reds speak loudest, sort others fast, and hate sitting still.

Well known Reds include Steve Jobs, Margaret Thatcher, and Mother Teresa. They lock in on a target and reach it. They decide fast, bend with change, and do not mind taking a side.

A useful note: more than 80 out of 100 people blend two styles, and only about 5 out of 100 lean on just one.

chapters.title: Each color carries its own mix of traits
chapters.text:
Hippocrates gave the Yellow type the name Sanguine. These people stay upbeat and cheerful, and they treat life like a bright painting. When those around them feel low, a Yellow keeps their hope alive. Yellows lift the room, find answers fast, and pull others along. They jump to decisions, even when the move is not fully thought out. Yellows build wide networks of friends and often have a knack for persuasion.

When plans fall apart, Yellows get resourceful and look for the next opening. They refuse to see walls, so they cast a wide net for choices. Well known Yellows include Oprah Winfrey, Ellen DeGeneres, and Robin Williams.

Greens sit at the calm middle of the four. They hold the louder traits in balance without losing their own voice. Hippocrates called this type phlegmatic, and they come across as steady and easygoing.

If every person on earth wanted to lead or hype others up, the whole place would tilt off balance.

Greens rarely grab attention, since they are quieter and more passive than the rest. They are simpler to work with, careful not to give offense, and quick to support others. They are kind, patient, and the best listeners in the room. Greens stick to their word, pull their weight in any team, and jump at chances to lend a hand. Well known Greens include Michelle Obama, Jimmy Carter, and Mahatma Gandhi.

We can head off the worst missteps by knowing the basics of human communication. ~ Thomas Erikson

The Blues study whatever is around them. They have a system for every task and tend to stick with it. A Blue type spots the risks and flaws in any plan. They keep their distance, notice small details, and lean toward a thoughtful mood.

Blues do not call attention to themselves and try to stay modest. They think in clear steps and believe that anything they do should be done well. Blues follow the rules and lean on what they already know. They weigh their words before speaking and handle tasks with care. Well known Blues include Bill Gates and Albert Einstein.

chapters.title: People act on the assumptions they already hold
chapters.text:
Some people are hard to read, and a few are nearly impossible. No one is perfect, and everyone has flaws.

Sometimes the people we call idiots are just people we have failed to understand.

Reds and Yellows play up their strengths and believe they have strong egos with no weak spots. They take feedback to heart and often read others the wrong way. Greens and Blues downplay their strengths and are more easygoing. When people give them feedback, they barely take it seriously.

Many people see Reds as arrogant, egotistical, and controlling. That is not always fair. Reds are blunt and direct. Reds are also the ones who feel most surrounded by idiots. Yellows are seen as selfish, shallow, and overconfident. They are also seen as careless and easily distracted.

Greens come across as pleasant, friendly, and caring. Because they are not pushy, they can be hard to read. Some people even see them as dishonest and stuck in their ways. Blues are seen as evasive, defensive, reserved, and cold. That comes from their analytical nature. Blues are also seen as suspicious.

Everyone deserves fair treatment, no matter the style. A fixed view of a color can do more harm than good. Take the time to study a person before you draw a conclusion.

chapters.title: Picking up new skills is a lifelong loop
chapters.text:
Mastering fresh ideas can be rough, because new concepts have to be taken in and decoded. It takes time, and no matter your line of work, reading people well speeds up the process. Curiosity plays a big part, since it is easier to stick with a topic that pulls you in. A surprising amount of friction fades once you see why people act the way they do.

You can pick up a lot about someone by watching how they carry themselves. Everyone gives off different signals when they speak or do a task, and others read those signals to gauge mood. Body language covers all forms of unspoken communication, on purpose or not. Even though body language has biological roots, culture and society shape it too.

A loose posture reads as self-assurance. A stiff, upright pose signals dominance. A slouched frame hints at disappointment. Darting eyes suggest distraction, and a level gaze reads as confidence. Head and face movements show agreement or disagreement. A firm handshake hints at a determined person, and a limp one can mark someone as yielding.

Body language does not mean the same thing to everyone, and keeping that in mind saves you from a lot of confusion.

Reds send clear physical signals. They stand at a distance, give firm handshakes, and hold direct eye contact. Yellows are looser, make friendly eye contact, and use lively gestures. Greens lean back, use small hand moves, and work at a steady pace. Blues keep their distance, hold direct eye contact, keep their arms and hands close, and speak without gestures.

A useful note: the English language has about 170,000 words, while body language has close to 700,000 signals.

chapters.title: Adaptation is a skill you need to get along
chapters.text:
It is good to be your real self when you talk to others, but sometimes you need to adapt. Everyone needs some emotional smarts to handle different kinds of people. Adapting can be hard and stressful, since it is natural to show your core behavior.

A Red wants you to do what they ask, right away. Adapting to a Red means moving faster. Be direct, stick to the main points, and share your opinion even when they are in a mood.

Healthy limits in any relationship build respect and understanding.

Yellows are not afraid of disagreement, but they do better in a warm setting. If you want the best from a Yellow, build a friendly space. They get distracted easily, so start with the important topics and even lay out a plan. Yellows are not afraid of risk, so show them you also follow your gut. Become friends and value the relationship. Stay approachable, smile often, and keep open body language.

A Green worries a lot and wants stability. Accept that they do not think like you, and listen to their concerns. Plan events for them, since they are not always ready to explore. If you have to criticize, do it in private, and watch your tone. Be patient while they find their way.

A Blue is detail-oriented, and that can be tiring. Be ready when you work with them. Use lists to make sure you are covering the right things at the right time. Win a Blue over with facts and solid research. It is fine to share how you feel and be honest with them.

chapters.title: Giving hard feedback takes skill
chapters.text:
Nobody likes bad news, but sometimes it has to be said. Feedback is easy to give the wrong way. A few tips can help you do it right.

Sharing bad news with a Red does not need much polish. They can react in many ways. Do not try to soften the situation. Give clear examples, stick to the facts, and be ready for sharp replies. Only give a Red feedback if you are ready for it. You have to be sure of yourself and ready for any attempt to flip the situation.

To give the right feedback, you need a solid read of the other person.

Yellows only accept criticism they have come up with themselves. If you want to give a Yellow hard feedback, set a clear plan and stick to it. Use examples and be ready for blunt comments. Explain that you dislike a behavior, not the person. Even though they are flexible, Yellows are the hardest to change. Stroke their ego and repeat what you said a few times.

Be gentle with a Green, but do not back down. Greens have softer egos and are often hard on themselves. Explain the problem and ask them to explain it back to you. It is easy to feel guilty giving a Green hard feedback, but it is sometimes needed.

A Blue is detail-oriented and will challenge any criticism fast. Make sure you know what you are saying. Give details in writing when you can, be ready for pushback, and stick to the facts.

chapters.title: Some people do not click, even when they try
chapters.text:
In a perfect world, the four colors would be evenly spread. That is not the case today. Different colors mix in different ways. Blues and Greens can work well together. They respect each other and feel safe, since they are both introverts. They do not panic and take time to explore options.

Reds and Yellows also work well. They share energy and are both loud and strong. Blues and Reds can be task-focused and process-driven. They are committed to the work and can round each other out. Green and Yellow work at different speeds but stay curious about each other. They find a balance, and the Green leaves space for the Yellow. Greens make good peacemakers, and the work gets done.

People work better together when they know each other strengths and weak spots.

A Red and a Green together can run into trouble. Greens are passive, and Reds are task-driven. The Reds are always in a hurry and will scold the Green for being too slow. The Greens will see the Reds as aggressive and push back. Another rough pair is Yellow and Blue. The Yellow starts a task without enough research but is excited. The Blue is careful and slow.

The Yellow will see the Blue as dull, and the Blue will see the Yellow as chatty.

In writing, Reds are short, Yellows are expressive and easygoing, Greens sit in the middle with a personal tone that stays brief, and Blues send notes packed with tasks and detail.

It is not easy to read everyone, since people carry many colors. If you cannot figure someone out, take your time and just listen.

chapters.title: Anger looks different for each color
chapters.text:
For Reds, very little sets them off. Traffic, a delayed order, or a missed call can be enough. When a Red gets angry, they let the feelings out and snap back to normal. They do not stay angry for long.

Yellows are expressive and emotional, and they get angry slowly. They do not hold grudges and forget there was a problem. Greens are patient, but once they lose their temper, it is hard to bring them back. They rarely release anger, and that may be why they face more anxiety and burnout. A Blue solves a problem before getting angry. They show their displeasure by grumbling quietly.

Sometimes stress is a cause of anger. Other times anger is the result of stress.

Reds blame everyone for their stress and are more demanding than other colors. If you lead a Red, tell them to get a grip or move their body. Yellows draw attention to themselves and become very upbeat. You can help them unwind by asking them to plan a party or shift their focus.

Greens are very private when stressed. They grow hesitant and unsure. Give them time alone to do what they like. Blues turn very pessimistic when stressed. Give them space to think.

In general, avoid piling stress on others and try to learn what sets each style off. That helps you build a healthy space at work or at home.

chapters.title: Wrapping up: read people and you will get further
chapters.text:
No team or family is made up of one type. If you want the group to do well, you need a mix of the colors. When different styles come together on a project, more ideas get shared. That leads to more output and growth for everyone.

A big part of good communication across styles is knowing what people already think. When you see that someone has a fixed idea about a topic, you can work around it. No matter how eager you are to be heard, be patient with the listener.

First, ask yourself which style you lean toward. Then look for ways to grow. It is hard to expect things from others if you have not tried to do them yourself. When you communicate, give people a break. Be kind, watch their body language, and be patient.

We are all different. Keep your eyes open, and you will see just how different. ~ Thomas Erikson

The people around you are not idiots. Like Sture, you have probably not taken the time to understand them. It may not be easy to read everyone, but you can start with one person. It is worth the effort.

Try this. Make a note to listen closely when others talk. You can write down what they say, repeat it to show you are paying attention, and ask questions when you need to. Also, study your family and coworkers to find out their styles.
